era_context: The Old Kingdom of Egypt is in its final decades; the Sixth Dynasty is fragmenting. The Akkadian Empire in Mesopotamia is under severe stress from climate, rebellion, and external pressure. The 4.2 ka climate event is at or near its peak. The Indus Valley Civilization is at its urban peak.

events.title: Egyptian Sixth Dynasty: Continued fragmentation; provincial autonomy increases
events.description: The Sixth Dynasty continues its decline. Provincial governors (nomarchs) consolidate power and resources, operating increasingly as independent rulers. Tomb inscriptions and administrative records show that these officials command their own military forces, maintain independent treasuries, and conduct their own foreign relations. The central government's authority is increasingly nominal. Archaeological evidence from provincial sites shows continued prosperity and construction activity at the local level, even as the royal court's power wanes. The pyramid-building programme has effectively ended.
key_figures: Pepi II (Sixth Dynasty pharaoh, traditional reign 2278–2184 BCE)
events.certainty_note: The fragmentation of the Sixth Dynasty is well-attested in the archaeological record. The precise year is uncertain.

events.title: Akkadian Empire: Peak of 4.2 ka climate crisis; famine and resource scarcity
events.description: The 4.2 ka climate event is at or near its peak intensity. Paleoclimate records show severe aridification across the Near East. Cuneiform records document famine, reduced agricultural output, and resource scarcity in Mesopotamia. The Akkadian Empire faces acute stress from climate-induced famine, military pressure from Gutian peoples, and internal rebellion. Administrative records show disruption of trade networks and loss of control over peripheral regions. The empire is fragmenting into regional power bases.

sources.name: Paleoclimate records (ice cores, tree rings, lake sediments)

events.certainty_note: The 4.2 ka climate event is well-documented in paleoclimate records. The impact on Mesopotamia is documented in cuneiform records.
cross_references: Contemporary with Egyptian Old Kingdom decline
Part of broader 4.2 ka climate crisis

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization: Mature urban phase; extensive trade networks
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization continues its mature phase without apparent disruption. Major cities show evidence of sophisticated urban planning, standardized weights and measures, and extensive trade networks. Archaeological stratigraphy shows continuous occupation and cultural continuity. The civilization appears resilient to the 4.2 ka climate event.
events.certainty_note: The Indus Valley Civilization's mature phase is well-established through archaeology.
cross_references: Contemporary with crises in Egypt and Mesopotamia
Shows regional variation in climate impact

disconfirming_evidence: The 4.2 ka climate event's global uniformity is questioned by the Indus Valley Civilization's apparent resilience. Some scholars argue that the Akkadian collapse was primarily driven by internal political factors rather than climate alone.
historiographic_note: This year falls within a period of sparse documentation. Egypt and Mesopotamia have written records, but these are often compiled much later. The Indus Valley has abundant archaeology but no deciphered writing. The 4.2 ka climate event is well-documented in paleoclimate records.
